Join your neighborhood associations for our 2nd Annual Spring Block Party! Family-friendly activities for all ages. Music, food, and games. Meet your neighbors, make new friends, and learn about what’s going on in our community, including at the New Era Community Garden!

There will also be a “Plant/Seedling Swap” for those who want to bring a plant and swap it for another. Bring your starts or plants you have too many of and swap them for something else.

Admission is FREE!

Hosted By: Marshall-New Era & Boulevard Park Neighborhood Associations

Who: All residents are welcome! You do not need to be a member of the neighborhood associations to attend.

Date: Saturday, April 18th

Time: 11am – 4pm (food truck too!)

Location: 26th St and C St cul-de-sac

Volunteer: We need volunteers! Click here if you can help out with the event, the link has several areas where we need assistance.

Resources Needed: We’re looking for a few items to help make the event successful: pop-up canopies, lemons, and non-toxic acrylic paint. If you can help, please email Quinn at qbuniel@gmail.com.


Transportation: We encourage neighbors to walk or bike to the party. Limited street parking is available near Leland Stanford Park, one block from the event.


Flyer: Download the event flyer here.

content.rendered:
Native Planting @ Sutter’s Landing
Saturday, February 28th | 9:30 AM
Join your neighbors and ReWild Sacramento on Saturday, February 28th at 9:30 AM to bring native plants back to Sutter’s Landing. We will also lay mulch, pull invasive weeds, and water the wildflowers we planted in our last event.

Click here to register for the event and get updates in the event of cancellation.

We will meet in the parking lot for the skate park, at the end of the road. Make sure to bring clothing suitable for the weather which can include cold, wind, rain or sun. It’s also recommended to bring a water bottle, gloves, and any tools you may find helpful. For questions, contact planttrees@rewildsacramento.org.

title.rendered: Beware of Marathon Delays Sunday, December 7th, 2025

content.rendered: The 42nd California International Marathon will be held Sunday, December 7th, 2025. The race begins in Folsom near the Folsom Dam and travels the same route as it has for forty-plus years along Oak Avenue, Fair Oaks Blvd, J Street and L Street, finishing at the Capitol. As in previous years, some intersections in or near our neighborhood will be impacted on that morning. J Street from Carlson to Alhambra; Alhambra from J Street to Capitol Ave and L Street from Alhambra to 15th Street will be closed from 8:00 am to 2:30 pm (approximately). The Capitol Mall and L Street around the Capitol will be closed from 3:00 am to 4:00 pm (approximately).

The Marshall New Era Neighborhood Association plans to clean up some of the traffic circles in our neighborhood, and plant new drought tolerant and native species plants later this fall. The first cleanup day will be Saturday Sept. 13th from 9am to 10:30am. We’ll meet at Marshall Park, at the corner of 27th and I Sts.

We’ll be starting with two traffic circles for this work event: at 25th and G Sts and 27th and F Sts. If enough volunteers step up to help, we’ll do additional traffic circles in the future.

Bring work gloves and water; we’ll provide garbage bags. Please RSVP to Mollie Quasebarth at molliequas@gmail.com if you can participate, so we can better plan our efforts.

content.rendered:
Sacramento residents are invited to learn new ways to save water and money at the Water Wise Garden Showcase on Sept. 27.

The free event will be held from 9 a.m. to noon at the Department of Utilities main campus (1395 35th Ave.)

“This event is about giving people the confidence and resources to make water-efficient improvements to their yards, no matter their skill level,” said William Granger, the City’s water conservation coordinator.

Attendees can connect one-on-one with gardening professionals, irrigation experts, landscape designers and contractors.

The event will also feature educational talks and information about rebates from the City’s water conservation team.

“We’re also excited to introduce some new features this year, including tours of our drought-tolerant garden, a native plant sale and even a mobile coffee bar,” said Granger.

Vendors expected to attend include:

  • City of Sacramento Water Conservation
  • City of Sacramento Recycling & Solid Waste
  • Master Gardeners of Sacramento County
  • Sacramento Tree Foundation
  • California Native Plant Society
  • Green Acres
  • GreenWaste Florin Perkins Landscape Materials
  • Hunter Industries
  • AM Landscape Design
  • Brodeur Landscapes
  • Pollinator Gardenworks
  • Landscape Symphonies
  • Mantis Gardens LLC
  • Prado + Jardin
  • Tranquill Gardens
  • Water-Wise Landscaping
  • Indulgence Mobile Coffee Company

Attendees will also have the chance to win prizes such as gift cards and free landscape design consultations.

People are encouraged to RSVP on the Department of Utilities’ Facebook page. A response is not required to attend but helps staff plan for the event.

title.rendered: Public review open for Two Rivers Trail Draft Environmental Report

content.rendered:
Plans to extend the Two Rivers Trail are continuing, and the City is inviting the community to review and provide comment on the recently released Draft Subsequent Environmental Impact Report (SEIR).

The proposed extension would add approximately one mile of new trail within Sutter’s Landing Park, starting at 28th Street and heading west. The new segment would loop around the Old City Landfill, just east of the Union Pacific Railroad (UPRR) tracks.

The project would also connect to the Sacramento Northern Trail west of UPRR, provide on-street bikeways along Basler Street and Sproule Avenue, and join the existing cycle track at North 12th Street and Sunbeam Avenue.

Future phases would include an overcrossing of the railroad tracks and an underpass beneath the State Route 160 bridges, ultimately connecting to the existing Two Rivers Trail.

The City selected the preferred alignment using input from public outreach meetings and surveys held in fall 2024. A Notice of Preparation meeting was held this spring and the Draft SEIR is now available for public review through Sept. 15.

“This next phase of the Two Rivers Trail is a vital step toward improving regional connectivity on our trails,” said Adam Randolph, project manager and senior engineer with the City’s Department of Public Works. “We’ve worked closely with the community to get this alignment right, and we look forward to hearing more feedback during the public comment period.”

A copy of the Draft SEIR is available for in-person review at the City’s Community Development Department, 300 Richards Blvd., third floor, between 9 a.m. and 4 p.m. (excluding holidays). It can also be accessed online on the City’s website.

A public meeting will be held via Zoom on Wednesday, Aug. 20, from 6-7 p.m. To join, visit: https://cityofsacramento-org.zoom.us/j/84117909326.

Written comments may be submitted to:

Charles Tschudin
Senior Planner
Community Development Department
300 Richards Blvd.
Sacramento, CA 95811
Email: ctschudin@cityofsacramento.org
Phone: (916) 808-8145

The proposed extension would add approximately one mile of new trail within Sutter’s Landing Park, starting at 28th Street and heading west. The new segment would loop around […]

content.rendered:
Celebrated on the first Tuesday of August each year, National Night Out was launched in 1984 with the goal of bringing communities together and strengthening relationships between residents and the local police. There will be block parties, festivals, cookouts, and other public events throughout Sacramento this year. The Boulevard Park and Marshall-New Era Neighborhood Associations will co-host a progressive potluck dinner with three courses at three different locations. Everyone is invited!

Here is the schedule for our event: Appetizer course on the 22nd Street median between G Street and Fat Alley (6:00 p.m.); dinner course in the courtyard beside 2431 H Street (7:00 p.m.); and dessert course near the tot-lot in Marshall Park, 28th and I Streets (8:00 p.m.). Bring a favorite dish to share at any one of the three courses. Beverages will be provided. Guests from outside our neighborhood will include City officials, elected representatives, and members of the police department.

Click for a flyer about the event that you can share with friends and neighbors.

You’re invited to CityConnect, our monthly mixer series! Join us every 3rd Thursday of the month to network with City staff, business owners, and fellow Sac residents. We’ll also be on-site to talk about our newest City programs! Admission & food will always be free to all attendees.

Where: The Lock & Key – Tapas + Bar (2718 J St, Sacramento, CA 95816) | Google Maps

When: Thursday, July 17th, 2025 from 5:30 – 8:30 PM

RSVP HERE: https://forms.office.com/g/ptgDw33Fuu

This July, we’re celebrating and looking back on the latest season of SacVibes, our local government podcast that aims to educate, engage, and empower Sacramento residents. Haven’t listened to SacVibes? Check out @ceteamsac on Youtube and Spotify!

Please RSVP so we can get an accurate headcount of how many people to expect. Attendees are encouraged to bring informational flyers to share at our resource table! Food and admission are always free.

The Community Engagement Team is committed to meeting folks where they are in order to share important City resources. Your attendance and support would be greatly appreciated! We also encourage you to share this invitation with your networks. If you have any questions or if you have a recommendation for where we should host our next mixer, please email ce@cityofsacramento.org. 

To stay up-to-date on everything related to Community Engagement, subscribe to our SacVibes vlog series and follow us on Instagram!

Community Engagement

915 I St, Sacramento, CA 95814

Stay Connected: Website | Instagram | Facebook | Linktree

title.rendered: Sage LaPena at Sutter’s Landing March 8th

content.rendered:
Sage LaPena will be sharing her knowledge of the plants and place of Sutters Landing on Saturday, March 8th. She has been in a long relationship with Sutters Landing and also holds understanding of its history through her indigenous persons lens. Sage LaPena is a Nomtipm Wintu ethnobotanist and Certified Medical Herbalist, a lecturer, teacher, and gardener specializing in both Native American and Western herbal traditions.

From the age of 7, Sage has been working with local medicine people from her tribe and other neighboring tribes. Sage maintains a strong connection with her tribe through continued participation in ceremonial and cultural activities.

She has been teaching “Ethnobotany of California native plants” for over twenty years and leads plant walks throughout the state. Her professional life is extensive as is her understanding of the land. You’ll find articles and interviews of Sage LaPena online. They provoke new thoughts and ways of being as we walk the trails along the rivers edge and elsewhere.

We’re grateful to her for guiding us on a plant walk at

Sutter’s Landing, March 8th from 10am until around noon.

We hope you can join us!

content.rendered: The Breathe California Sacramento Region’s 11thAnnual Public Service Announcement (PSA) Video Contest is open now through February 21, 2025!  All 6th -12th grade students who live or attend school within Sacramento County are encouraged to participate. Students are asked to put their creative skills to the test by developing a PSA video, up to 60 seconds in length, to raise awareness about the dangers of commercial tobacco use in their communities. Videos can be recorded using phones, cameras, laptops, or any other device. Winners will be announced at the Breathe Youth Fest on March 7, 2025, at the Sacramento Youth Center and will receive cash prizes!

content.rendered:
The City of Sacramento is inviting residents to weigh in on a proposed Parking Strategy, aimed at advancing housing and climate goals by updating parking requirements and optimizing parking management.

The strategy makes recommendations to:

  1. Improve public parking management
  2. Expand parking maximums
  3. Increase bicycle parking to support active transportation

Efforts to update and reconfigure mandates have been underway since 2013. The recently adopted 2040 Sacramento General Plan eliminated the minimum vehicle parking requirements. This is intended to prevent oversupply of parking and incentivizes more space for housing to reach housing goals.

Visit the Parking Strategy web page for more information.

The draft strategy is available for public comment until Monday, Dec. 2.

title.rendered: Accessible Afternoon Exploring the American River with Mobility Devices

content.rendered:
Participants and volunteers…. The Lionheart Community presents an afternoon exploring the American River with NatureTrack, McIllwain Mobility, and Paraboards.

Saturday, November 9, 2024 12:30-4:30PM
Sutter’s Landing Regional Park front of Skate Park
20 28th St, Sacramento, CA 95816
FREE EVENT!

Get ready for an epic day outdoors! Experience the thrill of hitting the trails and beach with NatureTrack’s fleet of Freedom Tracks! Plus, test drive McIlwain Mobility’s incredible lineup – from the rugged Eco Rover and Action Track Chair as well as the OMEO to the mind-blowing SwinCars. Catch speed in your manual chair with the new Paraboards! Don’t miss out!

Reserve your time slot on Eventbrite if you are interested in using the Freedom Trax with your manual wheelchair. All other equipment can be tried on a first come, first served basis. https://www.eventbrite.com/e/access-the-american-river-with-accessible-mobility-devices-tickets-1039379932357

title.rendered: Return of the Salmon Talk at Sutter’s Landing

content.rendered:
Come to The River. A Great Migration is Happening!

Witness the return of the Salmon and Hear their Story shared by Laura Drath of CDFW.

Hear the story of this great fish, its life journey and the centuries its kind have been traveling these waters. Discover how our lives are linked to one another now and throughout history on the rivers running through Sacramento. Bring your questions and curiosity. Laura will present and take questions at this family-friendly activity on the beach along the American River.

NOVEMBER 16, 2024

10:00 AM

Sutter’s Landing Beach

Sutter’s Landing, 20 28th Street, Sacramento, CA 95816

title.rendered: Input needed on potential routes to complete Two Rivers Trail

content.rendered:
The City of Sacramento is seeking feedback on the potential routes to complete the Two Rivers Trail system.

This phase of the project will extend the trail within Sutter’s Landing Park at 28th Street west, approximately one mile, to a loop around the Old City Landfill, just east of the Union Pacific Railroad (UPRR) tracks. West of the UPRR tracks, the trail will connect to the Sacramento Northern Trail and continue west to join the existing Two Rivers Trail.

“This will close the majority of the gaps in the trail and we’re looking for public feedback on their preferences on what the exact route should look like,” said Adam Randolph, project manager and senior engineer with the City’s Department of Public Works.

The survey on the alternative routes is available online here through Sept. 10.

The project will also explore alternatives to cross UPRR and State Route 160 to connect with the Two Rivers Trail. Environmental clearance, design and construction of the UPRR and State Route 160 crossings will likely be addressed in a future project.

excerpt.rendered: The City of Sacramento is seeking feedback on the potential routes to complete the Two Rivers Trail system. This phase of the project will extend the trail within Sutter’s Landing Park at 28th Street west, approximately one mile, to a loop around the Old City Landfill, just east of the Union Pacific Railroad (UPRR) tracks. West of the […]

title.rendered: McKinley Park Trash Dash Tomorrow August 25th

content.rendered:
Plan to come out this Sunday for our monthly Trash Dash, and this is our quarterly event sponsored by the City of Sacramento Recycling and Solid Waste division. This is a very family friendly event, and also a great opportunity for any local students to get community service credits.

Neighborhood Clean-up Grant Awardee: With the help of Friends of East Sacramento, MESA has received a grant from the city’s Recycling and Solid Waste division to support our Trash Dash quarterly. We will be focusing on the Northern Alhambra Corridor (B Street to J Street between 28th and Alhambra Blvd). This of course includes McKinley Park and Miwok Middle School. Our grant is $800 for this quarter and will cover supplies for our efforts and refreshments. This grant is intended to help clean up our local areas while also increasing recycling education and efforts. We’ll have trash grabbers and trash bags.

Come out, meet some new friends and make our neighborhood shiny. Our group will be out there from 10 am until noon, meeting up by the duck pond. Bring work gloves if you have them, and stay hydrated! For more information on this event please visit the Facebook page here: https://www.facebook.com/events/492248229945412/ and on Nextdoor here: https://nextdoor.com/p/2NBKBLyLdxZb?utm_source=share&extras=MTM0OTc2NjQ%3D&utm_campaign=1724528148500

And Coffee and Donuts, as well as Pizza is on MESA! With our grant we have coffee, donuts and pizza covered. An army of volunteers marches on their stomaches, so we want you well fed, energized and hydrated tomorrow. Bring along your thermal cup for hot coffee, hot chocolate or cold water, and stay after our Trash Dash for pizza on us.

This is a very family friendly event, and also a great opportunity for any local students to get community service credits. Neighborhood Clean-up Grant Awardee: With the […]

City Department of Utilities staff on Sept. 7 are inviting people to “Water Discovery Day,” an event designed to help people learn about how the City collects, treats and distributes drinking water.

The event will feature tours of the City’s Sacramento River Water Treatment Plant as well as booths with information on how the department operates.

Up to 260 people can register for the tours, which will be offered from 9 a.m. to noon. Registration is required to attend.*

Each tour lasts about 40 minutes and will educate attendees on several stages of the water treatment process.

Click here to learn more.

content.rendered:
Its that time of year, and not only is the weather heating up, but so are activities at Sutter’s Landing Park. Please join FOSL again this year and help us keep up the good work and many accomplishments we have achieved over the past 3 years. The Gateway Garden is in full bloom. The City of Sacramento is working on a mitigation project, and the Restoration team, led by Dan Meier, Tom Biglione, and Paul Barth is making extraordinary strides in bringing 3 acres of Parkway habitat back to life. There is lots to be proud of, but still a lot to be done.

Saturday, May 18th at 9:00 a.m.
The California Conservation Corps has made major strides these past couple of weeks knocking down the weeds and raking them out of the 3.2 acre restoration site. The native plants that survived are visible and in need of tending.

We have two main tasks to accomplish that are not too difficult but very rewarding. First, we will continue raking up the weeds the CCC crew cut over the past two weeks. Bring a small tarp and a rake if you can. We will move the weeds to piles beside the bike trail to be picked up by the County. Second, and very important, is to clean up the weeds growing on and close to the native plants – its detail work that the Corps could not get to.

All hands on deck mean the native plants will have the best opportunity to continue growing, and the area will be ready for planting more natives in the Fall. Yea!

Sunday, May 19th at 8:00 a.m.
This marks the official kick-off of our regular summer weekend clean-ups at Sutter’s Landing. Despite the fact that the River is running high, and the beaches are reduced in size, families of all sizes and shapes are arriving in numbers, as well as others seeking a respite from the heat and hustle of city life. So many people cannot stop marveling at how much SLP has changed. Thanks to the constant tending year-round – special thanks to Robert Sewell – the River and Parkway is staying safe and clean. This is not possible without dedicated volunteers, City and County Maintenance, and Park Rangers.

I hope we can continue to build on the weekend tending team that over the past three years have diligently picked up trash, emptied trash cans, removed invasive weeds, and kept an eye out for developing problems. These weekly tending parties are an excellent opportunity for individuals and groups looking for ways to preserve and protect the American River and Parkway. Help us spread the word.

IMPORTANT NOTE: Its a good idea to wear long pants and long sleeves, and most certainly closed toe shoes when working. Don’t forget a hat and sunscreen. FOSL will make sure we have plenty of water and snacks on hand.

We are looking forward to seeing old friends again, and making new ones. See you soon.

Be well and safe.

Your Parkway Pal,

Betsy Weiland, Volunteer Coordinator
Friends of Sutter’s Landing

content.rendered:
The City of Sacramento has launched the draft Sacramento Urban Forest Plan for public review and comment.

This document will become the City’s primary planning tool for the protection, expansion, maintenance, sustainability, and enhancement of trees in Sacramento.

“Trees are essential infrastructure that help our city function by helping clean our air and keep temperatures cool,” said Rachel Patten, the City’s Sustainability Program Specialist. “They provide many benefits to the community, so it’s important that we’re planning for them. The long-term health and success of the urban forest requires ongoing and intentional management and action.”

The draft plan is now live for review and comment through a self-guided online workshop on the City’s website. Comments will be accepted over the next two months and closes June 21.

The plan calls for increasing citywide tree canopy from 19 percent to 35 percent by 2045 which requires planting and maintaining an additional 25,000 trees per year.

Sacramento’s urban forest is made up of an estimated 1 million trees, extending across public property, private land, residential yards, parks, natural areas, streetscapes, schools, and commercial businesses throughout the city. Only about 100,000 are maintained by the City.

Planting efforts to hit the canopy goal will require engagement from the City, public, and other entities. Efforts will be prioritized in neighborhoods with the lowest canopy levels, highest heat exposure, and most socio-economically vulnerable residents.

The release of the draft plan aligns with the recent approval of the 2040 General Plan and Climate Action and Adaptation Plan (CAAP).

The CAAP outlines policies aimed at reducing carbon emissions and promoting sustainable development. It also establishes Sacramento’s greenhouse gas reduction target for 2030 and establishes a climate action goal of carbon neutrality by 2045.

City staff will attend and host events in neighborhoods across the city in the coming months to hear feedback on the draft plan. Stay up to date with the plan’s progress by signing up for email updates.

excerpt.rendered: The City of Sacramento has launched the draft Sacramento Urban Forest Plan for public review and comment. This document will become the City’s primary planning tool for the protection, expansion, maintenance, sustainability, and enhancement of trees in Sacramento. “Trees are essential infrastructure that help our city function by helping clean our air and keep temperatures cool,” said […]

title.rendered: River Banks Being Removed and Rebuilt

content.rendered:
Another stretch of our beautiful Wild and Scenic American River is at huge risk!

  • U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) has multiple “bank erosion” projects in progress (photos below show current work near Sac State). 
  • Contract “3B” is coming to the pristine areas east (upstream) of Howe/Watt all the way to Rio Bravo/Mayhew
  • Over 500 trees are planned to be bulldozed (may include beloved heritage oaks)
  • Massive damage done to the American River Parkway and wildlife habitat
  • More destruction than actual levee upgrades completed a decade ago
  • This new project would bring the total damaged area of the wildlife corridor to 11 miles out of the lower 26 miles of the Parkway! 
  • Despite the American River being a national and state Wild and Scenic River and portions designated as a “Protected Area” in the American River Parkway Plan, the USACE plans to move forward with the project . 
  • With more than 5 million visits annually to the American River Parkway, which is more than Yosemite, the project will diminish our parkway’s beauty and   recreational values.

To learn more go to https://www.americanrivertrees.org/

excerpt.rendered: Another stretch of our beautiful Wild and Scenic American River is at huge risk! To learn more go to https://www.americanrivertrees.org/

content.rendered:
Will you join us? It’s Clean Air Day on Oct. 4 and a great way to celebrate active transportation in our region.

Wednesday, October 4th at 9:00 am

Truitt Bark Park, 1818 Q Street

* Following program remarks, there will be an optional bike ride around the block to experience the new bikeways

If you are planning to attend and have not done so already, please RSVP to me or to midtownsacramentostation@gmail.com

The event will include remarks from Sacramento County Supervisor and Chair of SJJPA Pat Hume, Ripon Vice Mayor and Chair of SJRRC Leo Zuber, Sacramento Mayor Darrell Steinberg, Councilmember Katie Valenzuela, Assemblymember Stephanie Nguyen, Assemblymember Kevin McCarty, Jameson Parker from the Midtown Association, Megan Johnson and Phil Vulliet with the City of Sacramento, and former council member Steve Hansen. The event will also include information on the important nexus between active transportation, rail transportation, and affordable housing. Through its work on the Midtown Train Station,  the SJRRC successfully partnered with the City of Sacramento and provided $3 million in grant funding to support the bikeway extensions along 19th and 21st. SJRRC was also able to partner with EAH Housing to help secure grant funding to build out the bikeway network to the On Broadway 140-unit affordable housing project, which is less than a mile from the Midtown Station.

content.rendered:
You are invited to a screening of the movie Beyond Homeless at William Land Park this Saturday, August 12 from 7-10 pm, brought to you by Hope for Sacramento.  Hope for Sacramento is a local nonprofit working on a campus model shelter to provide services to Sacramento’s homeless.  Following the highly successful Haven for Hope model from San Antonio, Texas, Hope for Sacramento is planning a similar campus here.
Beyond Homeless is an award winning documentary that discusses the homelessness crisis our state is facing, and the model that is working to lift people out of homelessness in San Antonio.  This film was produced by Mary Theroux of the Independent Institute.  She has called California home for more than 40 years, residing in the San Francisco Bay Area.  She is working to help those experiencing homelessness and to create effective policies to solve the issues our communities are facing–always focusing on the people who are at the center of this crisis.  Mary is available for interviews.  You may reach her at (510) 632-1366 x108 or mtheroux@independent.org
Our city’s homeless crisis is touching all of us, both housed and unhoused.  Please come to our screening to learn more about the Haven for Hope project and to support Hope for Sacramento’s plans here in Sacramento.  Bring the family and some snacks for the movie screening.  We have great weather on tap–but those Delta breezes may be bringing some cool winds–so bring something to keep you warm!  

What:  Beyond Homeless documentary screening 

When:  August 12th from 7 pm to 10 pm

Where:  William Land Park Ampitheater, 3901 Land Park Dr, Sacramento, CA  95822

How:  Free screening provided by Hope for Sacramento

Why:  To learn about effective programs to provide shelter, services and assistance to those experiencing homelessness within our city.
To learn more about Hope for Sacramento, go to: https://hopeforsacramento.org/
Please share word of this screening with your friends, family, and communities. See you Saturday!

content.rendered:
Each year, the city of Sacramento creates a budget to plan how it will spend millions of taxpayer dollars on everyday services. 

The budget lays out city spending plans for everything from police staffing to homeless services to park projects. It also estimates how much revenue the city will receive for the financial year, which starts July 1 and ends June 30. 

Sacramento will hold public hearings on a proposed budget between May and June, inviting residents to give feedback before the City Council approves a several-hundred page document. 

But this time isn’t the only opportunity for residents to give input on how the city invests their money. Residents can talk to their elected representatives about their opinions on  budget priorities any time of the year, said assistant city manager Leyne Milstein. The city’s participatory budgeting pilot program has also allowed hundreds of people to get directly involved.

Read more of the Cap Radio article by clicking here

To learn more from the City website and to see the budget hearing schedule for this month click here.

This is a link to the Proposed Budget for 2023-2024.

It also estimates how much revenue the city will receive for the financial year, which […]

title.rendered: Invitation To Resident Meeting On May 17 For Nightlife

content.rendered:
Residents are invited to share their thoughts, concerns, and suggestions on our nighttime economy for the Sacramento Sociable City Assessment. The City’s Office of Nighttime Economy is partnering with Responsible Hospitality Institute (RHI), a non-profit dedicated to working with cities to build safe, vibrant, and well-managed social economies, to develop a nighttime management plan to enhance our nightlife and social economy.

Wednesday, May 17 at 5:30 pmTsakopoulos Library Galleria – 828 I Street

Nighttime businesses are part of our successful and vibrant nightlife, but they can impact the quality of life for neighborhoods. We want to hear from residents to help us establish priorities and goals. Please share the attached event flyer and encourage your members to join us for this meeting to provide their insight.

If you have any questions or need more information, please contact feel free to contact me. And please visit: www.sociablecity.solutions/sacramento/home for more details on the assessment.

Thanks,

Tina

Tina Lee-Vogt

City of Sacramento

Nighttime Economy Manager

Office of Innovation and Economic Development

Phone:  916-808-2679

Mobile: 916-207-3913

www.cityofsacramento.org/nighttime

nighttime@cityofsacramento.org

title.rendered: Join Us June 6th For The Sacramento 2040 Draft Plan

content.rendered:
Sacramento 2040 Draft General Plan Update is LIVE!

Join us via Zoom on Tuesday, June 6th at 10:00 am for our optional orientation Webinar.

Please join us, be a part of history, and help shape Sacramento!

The Draft Plan contains policies that guide everything from Environmental Justice to transportation, the type of homes available, jobs, entertainment, public safety, and much more. We would appreciate your feedback to ensure the Draft Plan is responsive to the challenges of the coming years.

This webinar continues the public review period of the document. During the 90-minute session, the project team will provide an overview of the General Plan (GP) and Climate Action & Adaptation Plan (CAAP), explain how the public can participate, and facilitate a Q&A session.

Your opinion matters! RSVP today!

(Translation channels will be available in Spanish and Chinese)

SIGN UP

Sacramento 2040 General Plan Update & Climate Action Plan is now live!

The Draft 2040 General Plan and Climate Action & Adaptation Plan are now available for public review via the Self-Guided Online Workshop.

﻿Visit the workshop today.

LEARN MORE

 ABOUT THE SACRAMENTO GENERAL PLAN UPDATE & CLIMATE ACTION PLAN

Sacramento 2040 GPU is the City’s blueprint for how and where our City will grow over the next 20 years. Throughout the outreach process, the Sacramento community identified a bold vision for a greener, more equitable, and more livable city. Participate in the virtual draft plan above to help help mold the future of Sacramento.

WWW.SAC2040GPU.ORG

content.rendered:
Who is Eligible to Apply:

  • Black, Indigenous and all People of Color who are emerging filmmakers
  • Absolutely no experience requiredNo location restrictions – we invite our global community to apply
  • Projects must have a strong central theme of bicycles

Annual Schedule

  • June 20 – Applications are due
  • Early July – All applications are notified via the email address provided in the application
  • December 10 – Rough cuts must be submitted by this date for review and feedback
  • January 20 – Films must be completed and submitted for the festival season
  • TBD – Films debut at the Filmed by Bike Film Festival in Portland

If You Are Awarded a Grant

  • Recipients will receive grants of $1,500 each
  • Number of grants awarded will be determined by financial contributions to the BIPOC Grant Program
  • Filmmakers have the option of being paired with an advisor for one-on-one support in bringing the film concept to the screen
  • Final films should be 25 minutes or shorter
  • Films created through this program will be presented in our Annual Filmed by Bike Festival in Portland, Oregon then go on tour around the world!

Application Guidelines

Questions? Please contact Melina@FilmedByBike.org. We can’t wait to read about your concept!

content.rendered:
Measure O, the Emergency Shelter and Enforcement Act of 2022 (ESEA) was passed by City of Sacramento voters in November 2022.  A key provisions of Measure O takes effect on June 21, 2023.  The ESEA creates a limited obligation on the City of Sacramento to expend resources to take specific actions to address the homelessness crisis in the City, subject to budget constraints and the establishment of a partnership agreement with the County of Sacramento. 

The City/County Partnership agreement is in place now.  Residents can file with the City Clerk a Notice of Violation and Demand to Abate that asks the City to abate unlawful camping violations or unlawful storage violations on public property. The City must inspect the location and respond to the resident within 20 days.

If the City refuses or fails to abate the violation, the resident may appeal the issue to a hearing examiner. If the hearing examiner orders the City to abate the violation, the City may be liable for the resident’s costs and attorney’s fees.

To read this and learn more, visit: https://www.cityofsacramento.gov/city-manager/projects-and-programs/measureo/faq

content.rendered: Get some exercise and meet your neighbors while caring for our community!  WHEN – July  29, 9:00 am-11:00am. Sign in and coffee & pastries from 8:30am-9:00am.  WHERE – Stanford Park on the corner of 28th and B Streets. HOW – We will walk our neighborhood picking up trash. We have trash pickers available for use or you can bring your own. Trash bags and gloves will be provided.  We would love to have you come out and improve our neighborhood! Please let us know if you plan on attending.

content.rendered:
Our next MNENA Board Meeting is Monday July 10th at 6:00 pm.  We’ll meet at the Society Church, 2711 B St, Sacramento (where B Street Theater used to be).  They have been very kind to offer their building for our meetings.

As usual we will have a light potluck before we start the meeting so please bring something to share if you like, or just bring your questions and socialize with us.  Masks are not required but feel free to wear one if you are more comfortable doing so.  No need to RSVP, just show up.  

Here is a draft agenda and the draft minutes from the May meeting.

Hope to see you there,

The MNENA Board
